comment_content: What! Season 3 coming back?

Most people miss all the hidden biblical allegory the writer secretly slipped into the entire show. Player 001 can be referred to as Lucifer (the one seated at the top who rules over the pyramid) who represents the first and player 456 (as jesus who returns again to help save people trapped in lucifers bondage by helping them come outve bondage or the system of greed, debt and temptation) and who represents the last. Lucifer comes down from his high places of rule and enters into the game to balance the scales and work of jesus. They both are on a mission (riding white horses). One seeks to prove how humans are inferior, sinful and selfish... and deserve to suffer. The other seeks to prove how humans can repent and become better people, and who can become selfless by sacrificing themselves for others and therefor deserve to be forgiven and passed over in wrath. One hides behind a mask (as does his minions) representing his power as the "covering" cherub that opperates in secret through his occult. The other comes and brings truth about the system, what it is and how to come outve it by following him. He never gives up hope for humanity even though he comes close several times because so many are lost in their own greed and sin. Yet he stands up for the pure of heart and fights for them. At every turn, the front man (or first player) fights against him mentally to undo all of the work he does. Its a spiritual battle between the one who believes in the small remnant pure of heart vs the one who has lost faith in humanity and now believes in the larger group of evil people. The front man was once good and lost his humanity by having his heart (or morality) become corrupted by his logic and reasoning. He's very clever at weighing options between two dual paths (tree of knowledge of good and evil) but is terrible at using his love to find the 3rd option from the tree of life. Theres so much more hidden throughout the show that everyone misses. But you get the picture.
That's why I hate trailers, it spoils everything before watching the movie! 😒

comment_content: To those who hated the ending… i found someone comment this so im sharing.. This made me realize Gi-hun's final act wasn't just a rejection of the game. It was a rejection of the philosophy that created it. The Front Man believed humans are inherently selfish, driven by survival and greed, no better than horses forced to run for others’ amusement. But Gi-hun proved that wrong by doing the one thing no one else could refusing to play, even if it meant dying. His choice wasn’t strategic. It wasn’t for reward. It was to preserve something bigger than himself, something like compassion, dignity, and choice. His last words, "We are not horses. We are humans. Humans are..." The silence afterward says everything. It’s not unfinished by accident. It’s a question, a challenge, maybe even a warning. How do you define human after everything we’ve seen? The games showed us the worst. Betrayal. Cruelty. Cowardice. But they also showed us sacrifice, love, and resistance. So what are humans? Are we good? Evil? Selfish? Kind? Maybe we’re all of it at once. Gi-hun’s story doesn’t give us a clean answer. It forces you to ask what being human even means. Is humanity just compassion? If so, what about the people who created the games? They were human too. The word humanity starts to lose meaning when you realize the same species capable of kindness is also capable of horror. In the end, Gi-hun didn’t try to fix humanity. He just chose to be the kind of human he believed should exist. Not perfect. Not heroic. Just human. And maybe that’s the only answer there is.
reply_content: I loved the ending. It wasn't a matter of winning, the whole series is a study on morals. He didn't want to be a murderer, and stuck to it.

It was a rejection of the philosophy that created it. The Front Man believed humans are inherently selfish, driven by survival and greed, no better than horses forced to run for others’ amusement. But Gi-hun proved that wrong by doing the one thing no one else could refusing to play, even if it meant dying. His choice wasn’t strategic. It wasn’t for reward. It was to preserve something bigger than himself, something like compassion, dignity, and choice. His last words, "We are not horses. We are humans. Humans are..." The silence afterward says everything. It’s not unfinished by accident. It’s a question, a challenge, maybe even a warning. How do you define human after everything we’ve seen? The games showed us the worst. Betrayal. Cruelty. Cowardice. But they also showed us sacrifice, love, and resistance. So what are humans? Are we good? Evil? Selfish? Kind? Maybe we’re all of it at once. Gi-hun’s story doesn’t give us a clean answer. It forces you to ask what being human even means. Is humanity just compassion? If so, what about the people who created the games? They were human too. The word humanity starts to lose meaning when you realize the same species capable of kindness is also capable of horror. In the end, Gi-hun didn’t try to fix humanity. He just chose to be the kind of human he believed should exist. Not perfect. Not heroic. Just human. And maybe that’s the only answer there is.

reply_content:
Agreed, this is how it was supposed to be interpreted. But to be fair, the problem with this "Gi-hun sacrifices himself for the baby" ending is that both In-ho and the audience have seen multiple times throughout S2/S3 that Gi-hun has already given up on life (from playing Russian rulette with the Recruiter in S2Ep1 to literally begging In-ho to end him in S3Ep1). The baby was the only reason that motivated him to continue on after the rebellion failed. 

Not to mention his daughter, ex-wife and mother are all gone. Gi-hun had nothing waiting for him in the outside world, so why on earth would someone as empathetic and kind-hearted as him want to win the games a second time? I get the show tried to say Gi-hun proved In-ho wrong and the baby symbolizes hope, but are the standards for humanity really so low that it's considered to be a great miracle that an adult is willing to sacrifice themselves for a literal newborn? Besides, not once has In-ho claimed that humans can´t do good things, he himself saved his own brother which was against the rules, but it doesn´t change the fact he sees players as "trash". And based on their meeting in S3, he doesn´t see Gi-hun as "trash" unlike the other players.

Not to mention the show strongly implied the baby may remind In-ho of his deceased wife and unborn child (which is a backstory that was created solely for S2/S3, since in S1 his player file makes no mention of him having a wife), so doesn´t it unintentionally suggest that the antagonist wouldn´t have cared at all without this tragic backstory? Better way to prove In-ho wrong imo would´ve been, if there was no baby plot in S2/S3, if Gi-hun sacrificed himself for Myung-gi, and he did it even if Myung-gi didn´t succeed at being a perfect person during these cruel games.

reply_content:
Stop yapping. Stop insisting the ending was good. There’s no need to force positive reasons onto a trash finale. Proving humanity exists doesn’t require Gi-hun to throw his life away for a CGI baby. What kind of nonsensical ending is that?

Humans are complex they can be evil, selfish, greedy, compassionate, selfless, cruel, or kind. They make mistakes, they learn, they manipulate, they sacrifice but it all depends on context. The answers are many; human nature cannot be simplified or explained by a single exaggerated act.

This finale ignored all of that. It threw subtlety, logic, and character development out the window. Instead of showing the philosophy of humanity through relationships, choices, and real moral dilemmas, it forced a pointless sacrifice that adds nothing to the story. It’s lazy, it’s illogical, and it’s a disservice to everything the series built. Trash doesn’t even begin to describe it.

comment_content:
I think the story is about a loop that happens every 4 years.

The owner of the game is cursed because he created such a dangerous game. Now, he wants to end it. But the game can only end if Player 456 stays alive until the end of the 4th year.

Only two people are stuck in this loop: the game's owner and the daughter of Player 456.

The daughter is the same girl we see at the end — trying to destroy the game's documents. She also sees her father die while sitting on the couch.

Now the secret is out!

Text:
Die letzte Staffel von Squid Game ist ein absolutes Desaster. Völlig sinnlose Story, peinlich schlechte Dialoge und null Spannung. Es wirkt, als wäre den Machern alles egal gewesen – Hauptsache, schnell irgendwas zusammenschustern, um noch Geld zu kassieren. Die Figuren benehmen sich unlogisch, jede Folge zieht sich wie Kaugummi, und das Finale ist einfach nur ein Witz. Ich habe mich selten so gelangweilt und gleichzeitig geärgert, meine Zeit damit verschwendet zu haben. Netflix sollte sich schämen, so einen lieblosen Müll zu veröffentlichen. Absolut nicht empfehlenswert.
So eine scheiße Grösste mistgeburtenletzte folge 3 Stunden meiner Lebenszeit verschwendet nur damit der Affe runterspringt
